---
title: "Wooldridge Introductory Econometrics -- Ch. 13: Pooling cross sections across time (via StatEcon)"
Authors:
- Jeffrey M. Wooldridge
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

**Source.** Stata code and output reproduced from Wooldridge, J. M.,
*Introductory Econometrics: A Modern Approach* (Cengage), Chapter 13:
*Pooling Cross Sections across Time: Simple Panel Data Methods*.

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq

# Artifacts this notebook produces go to `output/`.
const OUT = "output"
isdir(OUT) || mkpath(OUT)
outpath(f) = joinpath(OUT, f)
```

All models in this chapter are pooled-OLS regressions on cross sections
stacked over time. The interaction, difference, and year-dummy variables the
book uses (`y85educ`, `y81nrinc`, `cslpnap`, …) already ship inside the Stata
`.dta` files, so we regress on them directly. Datasets are loaded by bare name;
`injury` collides with the panel-data set of the same name, so it is qualified
`wooldridge_intro/injury`.

# Example 13.1: Woman's Fertility Over Time

```{julia}
#=
use "data/fertil1.dta", clear
reg kids educ age agesq black east northcen west farm othrural town smcity y74 y76 y78 y80 y82 y84

      Source |       SS           df       MS      Number of obs   =     1,129
-------------+----------------------------------   F(17, 1111)     =      9.72
       Model |  399.610888        17  23.5065228   Prob > F        =    0.0000
    Residual |  2685.89841     1,111  2.41755033   R-squared       =    0.1295
-------------+----------------------------------   Adj R-squared   =    0.1162
       Total |   3085.5093     1,128  2.73538059   Root MSE        =    1.5548

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
        kids | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
        educ |  -.1284268   .0183486    -7.00   0.000    -.1644286    -.092425
         age |   .5321346   .1383863     3.85   0.000     .2606065    .8036626
       agesq |   -.005804   .0015643    -3.71   0.000    -.0088733   -.0027347
       black |   1.075658   .1735356     6.20   0.000     .7351631    1.416152
        east |    .217324   .1327878     1.64   0.102    -.0432192    .4778672
    northcen |    .363114   .1208969     3.00   0.003      .125902    .6003261
        west |   .1976032   .1669134     1.18   0.237    -.1298978    .5251041
        farm |  -.0525575     .14719    -0.36   0.721    -.3413592    .2362443
    othrural |  -.1628537    .175442    -0.93   0.353    -.5070887    .1813814
        town |   .0843532    .124531     0.68   0.498    -.1599893    .3286957
      smcity |   .2118791    .160296     1.32   0.187    -.1026379    .5263961
         y74 |   .2681825    .172716     1.55   0.121    -.0707039    .6070689
         y76 |  -.0973795   .1790456    -0.54   0.587     -.448685    .2539261
         y78 |  -.0686665   .1816837    -0.38   0.706    -.4251483    .2878154
         y80 |  -.0713053   .1827707    -0.39   0.697      -.42992    .2873093
         y82 |  -.5224842   .1724361    -3.03   0.003    -.8608214    -.184147
         y84 |  -.5451661   .1745162    -3.12   0.002    -.8875846   -.2027477
       _cons |  -7.742457   3.051767    -2.54   0.011    -13.73033   -1.754579
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
fertil1 = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("fertil1"))   # 1129 x 27
m131 = stata_regress(fertil1; y = :kids,
    x = [:educ, :age, :agesq, :black, :east, :northcen, :west, :farm,
         :othrural, :town, :smcity, :y74, :y76, :y78, :y80, :y82, :y84])
```

```{julia}
#=
test y74 y76 y78 y80 y82 y84

       F(  6,  1111) =    5.87
            Prob > F =    0.0000
=#
stata_test(m131, ["y74", "y76", "y78", "y80", "y82", "y84"])
```

Exact reproduction. The year dummies are jointly significant (`F(6, 1111) =
5.87`), and the negative trend after 1980 shows fertility falling relative to
the base year even after controlling for education and background.

# Example 13.2: Changes in the Return to Education and the Gender Wage Gap

```{julia}
#=
use "data/cps78_85.dta", clear
reg lwage y85 educ y85educ exper expersq union female y85fem

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
       lwage | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
         y85 |   .1178062   .1237817     0.95   0.341     -.125075    .3606874
        educ |   .0747209   .0066764    11.19   0.000     .0616206    .0878212
     y85educ |   .0184605   .0093542     1.97   0.049      .000106     .036815
       exper |   .0295843   .0035673     8.29   0.000     .0225846     .036584
     expersq |  -.0003994   .0000775    -5.15   0.000    -.0005516   -.0002473
       union |   .2021319   .0302945     6.67   0.000     .1426888    .2615749
      female |  -.3167086   .0366215    -8.65   0.000    -.3885663    -.244851
      y85fem |    .085052    .051309     1.66   0.098    -.0156251     .185729
       _cons |   .4589329   .0934485     4.91   0.000     .2755707     .642295
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
R-squared = 0.4262 ; Root MSE = .4127 ; N = 1,084
=#
cps = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("cps78_85"))   # 1084 x 15
m132 = stata_regress(cps; y = :lwage,
    x = [:y85, :educ, :y85educ, :exper, :expersq, :union, :female, :y85fem])
```

Exact reproduction. The return to a year of schooling rose by `.0185` between
1978 and 1985, while the gender gap (`female`) narrowed by `.085` (the `y85fem`
interaction), though only marginally significant.

# Example 13.3: Effect of a Garbage Incinerator's Location on Housing Prices

The separate-year regressions illustrate why the difference-in-differences
coefficient equals the difference of the two `nearinc` slopes.

```{julia}
#=
use "data/kielmc.dta", clear
reg rprice nearinc if year==1981

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      rprice | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
     nearinc |  -30688.27   5827.709    -5.27   0.000    -42209.97   -19166.58
       _cons |   101307.5   3093.027    32.75   0.000     95192.43    107422.6
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
N = 142 ; R-squared = 0.1653
=#
kielmc = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("kielmc"))   # 321 x 25
k81 = kielmc[kielmc.year .== 1981, :]
m81 = stata_regress(k81; y = :rprice, x = [:nearinc])
```

```{julia}
#=
scalar b1=_b[nearinc]
reg rprice nearinc if year==1978

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      rprice | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
     nearinc |  -18824.37   4744.594    -3.97   0.000    -28187.62   -9461.117
       _cons |   82517.23    2653.79    31.09   0.000     77280.09    87754.37
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
N = 179 ; R-squared = 0.0817
=#
k78 = kielmc[kielmc.year .== 1978, :]
m78 = stata_regress(k78; y = :rprice, x = [:nearinc])
```

```{julia}
#=
scalar b2=_b[nearinc]
display b1-b2
-11863.903
=#
b1 = StatsBase.coef(m81)[findfirst(==("nearinc"), string.(StatsBase.coefnames(m81)))]
b2 = StatsBase.coef(m78)[findfirst(==("nearinc"), string.(StatsBase.coefnames(m78)))]
Printf.@printf("b1 - b2 = %.3f\n", b1 - b2)
```

```{julia}
#=
reg rprice nearinc y81 y81nrinc

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      rprice | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
     nearinc |  -18824.37   4875.322    -3.86   0.000    -28416.45   -9232.293
         y81 |   18790.29   4050.065     4.64   0.000     10821.88    26758.69
    y81nrinc |   -11863.9   7456.646    -1.59   0.113    -26534.67    2806.867
       _cons |   82517.23    2726.91    30.26   0.000      77152.1    87882.36
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
m133a = stata_regress(kielmc; y = :rprice, x = [:nearinc, :y81, :y81nrinc])
```

```{julia}
#=
reg rprice nearinc y81 y81nrinc age agesq

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      rprice | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
     nearinc |   9397.936   4812.222     1.95   0.052    -70.22385     18866.1
         y81 |   21321.04   3443.631     6.19   0.000     14545.62    28096.47
    y81nrinc |  -21920.27   6359.745    -3.45   0.001    -34433.22   -9407.321
         age |  -1494.424   131.8603   -11.33   0.000    -1753.862   -1234.986
       agesq |   8.691277   .8481268    10.25   0.000     7.022567    10.35999
       _cons |   89116.54   2406.051    37.04   0.000     84382.57     93850.5
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
m133b = stata_regress(kielmc; y = :rprice,
    x = [:nearinc, :y81, :y81nrinc, :age, :agesq])
```

```{julia}
#=
reg rprice nearinc y81 y81nrinc age agesq intst land area rooms baths

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      rprice | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
     nearinc |   3780.337   4453.415     0.85   0.397    -4982.408    12543.08
         y81 |   13928.48   2798.747     4.98   0.000     8421.533    19435.42
    y81nrinc |  -14177.93   4987.267    -2.84   0.005    -23991.11   -4364.759
         age |   -739.451   131.1272    -5.64   0.000    -997.4629   -481.4391
       agesq |    3.45274   .8128214     4.25   0.000     1.853395    5.052084
       intst |  -.5386352   .1963359    -2.74   0.006    -.9249548   -.1523157
        land |   .1414196   .0310776     4.55   0.000     .0802698    .2025693
        area |   18.08621   2.306064     7.84   0.000     13.54869    22.62373
       rooms |   3304.227   1661.248     1.99   0.048     35.47904    6572.974
       baths |   6977.317   2581.321     2.70   0.007     1898.191    12056.44
       _cons |   13807.67   11166.59     1.24   0.217    -8164.239    35779.57
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
m133c = stata_regress(kielmc; y = :rprice,
    x = [:nearinc, :y81, :y81nrinc, :age, :agesq, :intst, :land, :area,
         :rooms, :baths])
```

```{julia}
#=
reg lprice nearinc y81 y81nrinc

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      lprice | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
     nearinc |   -.339923   .0545555    -6.23   0.000    -.4472595   -.2325865
         y81 |   .4569953   .0453207    10.08   0.000     .3678279    .5461627
    y81nrinc |   -.062649   .0834408    -0.75   0.453    -.2268167    .1015187
       _cons |   11.28542   .0305145   369.84   0.000     11.22539    11.34546
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
m133d = stata_regress(kielmc; y = :lprice, x = [:nearinc, :y81, :y81nrinc])
```

All six regressions reproduce exactly. The pooled `y81nrinc` slope in the raw
model (`-11863.9`) equals the difference `b1 - b2` of the separate-year `nearinc`
slopes, and once `age`/`agesq` (and house characteristics) are controlled the
incinerator effect grows to `-21920` / `-14178`.

# Example 13.4: Effect of Worker Compensation Laws on Duration

```{julia}
#=
use "data/injury.dta", clear
reg ldurat afchnge highearn afhigh if ky

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      ldurat | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
     afchnge |   .0076573   .0447173     0.17   0.864    -.0800058    .0953204
    highearn |   .2564785   .0474464     5.41   0.000     .1634652    .3494918
      afhigh |   .1906012   .0685089     2.78   0.005     .0562973    .3249051
       _cons |   1.125615   .0307368    36.62   0.000     1.065359    1.185871
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
N = 5,626 ; R-squared = 0.0207
=#
injury = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("wooldridge_intro/injury"))   # qualified
inj_ky = injury[injury.ky .== 1, :]
m134 = stata_regress(inj_ky; y = :ldurat, x = [:afchnge, :highearn, :afhigh])
```

Exact reproduction. The difference-in-differences coefficient `afhigh = .1906`
implies the higher benefit cap raised the duration of high-earners' claims by
roughly 19% after the policy change.

# Example 13.5: Sleeping Versus Working

```{julia}
#=
use "data/slp75_81.dta", clear
reg cslpnap ctotwrk ceduc cmarr cyngkid cgdhlth

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     cslpnap | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
     ctotwrk |  -.2266694    .036054    -6.29   0.000    -.2977029   -.1556359
       ceduc |  -.0244717   48.75938    -0.00   1.000    -96.09008    96.04113
       cmarr |   104.2139   92.85536     1.12   0.263    -78.72946    287.1574
     cyngkid |    94.6654   87.65252     1.08   0.281    -78.02739    267.3582
     cgdhlth |   87.57785   76.59913     1.14   0.254    -63.33758    238.4933
       _cons |  -92.63404    45.8659    -2.02   0.045    -182.9989   -2.269152
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
N = 239 ; R-squared = 0.1495
=#
slp = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("slp75_81"))   # 239 x 20
m135 = stata_regress(slp; y = :cslpnap,
    x = [:ctotwrk, :ceduc, :cmarr, :cyngkid, :cgdhlth])
```

```{julia}
#=
test ceduc cmarr cyngkid cgdhlth

       F(  4,   233) =    0.86
            Prob > F =    0.4857
=#
stata_test(m135, ["ceduc", "cmarr", "cyngkid", "cgdhlth"])
```

Exact reproduction of this first-differenced sleep equation. Only the trade-off
with work (`ctotwrk = -.227`) is significant; the other differenced controls are
jointly insignificant (`F(4, 233) = 0.86`).

# Example 13.6: Distributed Lag of Crime Rate on Clear-up Rate

```{julia}
#=
use "data/crime3.dta", clear
reg clcrime cclrprc1 cclrprc2

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     clcrime | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
    cclrprc1 |  -.0040475   .0047199    -0.86   0.395    -.0135276    .0054326
    cclrprc2 |  -.0131966   .0051946    -2.54   0.014    -.0236302   -.0027629
       _cons |   .0856556   .0637825     1.34   0.185    -.0424553    .2137665
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
N = 53 ; R-squared = 0.1933
=#
crime3 = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("crime3"))   # 106 x 12
m136 = stata_regress(crime3; y = :clcrime, x = [:cclrprc1, :cclrprc2])
```

Exact reproduction. The (differenced) second lag of the clear-up rate has a
significant deterrent effect on crime growth.

# Example 13.7: Effect of Drunk Driving Laws on Traffic Fatalities

```{julia}
#=
use "data/traffic1.dta", clear
reg cdthrte copen cadmn

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     cdthrte | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
       copen |  -.4196787   .2055948    -2.04   0.047    -.8330547   -.0063028
       cadmn |  -.1506024   .1168223    -1.29   0.204    -.3854894    .0842846
       _cons |  -.4967872   .0524256    -9.48   0.000    -.6021959   -.3913784
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
N = 51 ; R-squared = 0.1187
=#
traffic1 = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("traffic1"))   # 51 x 13
m137 = stata_regress(traffic1; y = :cdthrte, x = [:copen, :cadmn])
```

Exact reproduction. Open-container laws (`copen`) significantly lowered the
change in the traffic death rate; administrative per-se laws (`cadmn`) less so.

# Example 13.8: Effect of Enterprise Zones on Unemployment Claims

```{julia}
#=
use "data/ezunem.dta", clear
reg guclms d82 d83 d84 d85 d86 d87 d88 cez

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      guclms | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
         d82 |   .7787595   .0651444    11.95   0.000     .6501469    .9073721
         d83 |  -.0331192   .0651444    -0.51   0.612    -.1617318    .0954934
         d84 |  -.0171382   .0685455    -0.25   0.803    -.1524655    .1181891
         d85 |    .323081   .0666774     4.85   0.000     .1914417    .4547202
         d86 |    .292154   .0651444     4.48   0.000     .1635413    .4207666
         d87 |   .0539481   .0651444     0.83   0.409    -.0746645    .1825607
         d88 |  -.0170526   .0651444    -0.26   0.794    -.1456652    .1115601
         cez |  -.1818775   .0781862    -2.33   0.021    -.3362382   -.0275169
       _cons |  -.3216319    .046064    -6.98   0.000    -.4125748   -.2306891
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
N = 176 ; R-squared = 0.6230
=#
ezunem = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("ezunem"))   # 176 x 38
m138 = stata_regress(ezunem; y = :guclms,
    x = [:d82, :d83, :d84, :d85, :d86, :d87, :d88, :cez])
```

```{julia}
#=
bpagan d82 d83 d84 d85 d86 d87 d88 cez

Breusch-Pagan LM statistic:   6.58428  Chi-sq( 8)  P-value =  .5821
=#
estat_hettest(m138, ezunem;
    vars = [:d82, :d83, :d84, :d85, :d86, :d87, :d88, :cez])
```

The pooled first-differenced model reproduces exactly (`cez = -.1819`: enterprise
zones cut the growth of unemployment claims by ~18%). The user-written `bpagan`
reports the *original* Breusch-Pagan (1979) statistic — the score form
`ESS/(2σ̂⁴)` from regressing squared residuals on the eight regressors — which is
exactly `estat_hettest`'s default (Normal/score) form: `LM = 6.58428`, `Chi²(8)`,
`p = .582`, matching Stata to five decimals. (The Koenker `n·R²` variant,
`estat_hettest(…, iid = true)`, would instead give 6.914.)

# Example 13.9: County Crime Rates in North Carolina

```{julia}
#=
use "data/crime4.dta", clear
reg clcrmrte d83 d84 d85 d86 d87 clprbarr clprbcon clprbpri clavgsen clpolpc

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    clcrmrte | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
         d83 |  -.0998658   .0238953    -4.18   0.000    -.1468071   -.0529246
         d84 |  -.0479374   .0235021    -2.04   0.042    -.0941063   -.0017686
         d85 |  -.0046111   .0234998    -0.20   0.845    -.0507756    .0415533
         d86 |   .0275143   .0241494     1.14   0.255    -.0199261    .0749548
         d87 |   .0408267   .0244153     1.67   0.095    -.0071361    .0887895
    clprbarr |  -.3274942   .0299801   -10.92   0.000    -.3863889   -.2685995
    clprbcon |  -.2381066   .0182341   -13.06   0.000    -.2739268   -.2022864
    clprbpri |  -.1650462    .025969    -6.36   0.000    -.2160613   -.1140312
    clavgsen |  -.0217607   .0220909    -0.99   0.325    -.0651574     .021636
     clpolpc |   .3984264    .026882    14.82   0.000     .3456177     .451235
       _cons |   .0077134   .0170579     0.45   0.651    -.0257961    .0412229
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
N = 540 ; R-squared = 0.4325
=#
crime4 = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("crime4"))   # 630 x 59
m139 = stata_regress(crime4; y = :clcrmrte,
    x = [:d83, :d84, :d85, :d86, :d87, :clprbarr, :clprbcon, :clprbpri,
         :clavgsen, :clpolpc])
```

```{julia}
#=
whitetst
White's general test statistic :  257.5729  Chi-sq(50)  P-value =  1.0e-29
=#
imt139 = estat_imtest(m139, crime4)
imt139
```

The differenced county-crime equation reproduces exactly. `estat_imtest` provides
White's general heteroskedasticity test (the `whitetst` command is a special
case): its heteroskedasticity component is `257.57296`, reproducing Stata's
`White's general test statistic = 257.5729` **exactly**. The only difference is
the reported degrees of freedom — `estat_imtest` counts 60, `whitetst` counts 50
— because the two commands drop a different number of redundant square/cross-
product terms from the auxiliary regression; both p-values are ≈0 and reject
homoskedasticity decisively.

# Verification summary

| Example | Quantity | Stata | Julia | Match |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 13.1 | `educ` | -.1284268 / .0183486 | same | yes |
| 13.1 | R² / N | 0.1295 / 1129 | same | yes |
| 13.1 | `test` year dummies | F(6,1111)=5.87 | F=5.87 | yes |
| 13.2 | `y85educ` | .0184605 / .0093542 | same | yes |
| 13.2 | `female` | -.3167086 / .0366215 | same | yes |
| 13.2 | R² | 0.4262 | 0.4262 | yes |
| 13.3 | `nearinc` (1981) | -30688.27 / 5827.709 | same | yes |
| 13.3 | `nearinc` (1978) | -18824.37 / 4744.594 | same | yes |
| 13.3 | b1 − b2 | -11863.903 | -11863.903 | yes |
| 13.3 | `y81nrinc` (pooled) | -11863.9 / 7456.646 | same | yes |
| 13.3 | `y81nrinc` (+age,+char) | -14177.93 / 4987.267 | same | yes |
| 13.4 | `afhigh` | .1906012 / .0685089 | same | yes |
| 13.5 | `ctotwrk` | -.2266694 / .036054 | same | yes |
| 13.5 | `test` 4 diffs | F(4,233)=0.86, p=.4857 | same | yes |
| 13.6 | `cclrprc2` | -.0131966 / .0051946 | same | yes |
| 13.7 | `copen` | -.4196787 / .2055948 | same | yes |
| 13.8 | `cez` | -.1818775 / .0781862 | same | yes |
| 13.8 | `bpagan` LM | 6.58428 / Chi²(8) / .5821 | 6.58428 / Chi²(8) / .5821 | yes |
| 13.9 | `clpolpc` | .3984264 / .026882 | same | yes |
| 13.9 | White test stat | 257.5729 | 257.57296 | yes |
| 13.9 | White test df | Chi²(50) | Chi²(60) (imtest) | df conv. |

All pooled-OLS coefficients, standard errors, R², and the `test` F-statistics
reproduce Stata's `reg` output exactly (StatEcon's `stata_regress` uses the same
OLS estimator and small-sample t/F distribution as Stata). The two
heteroskedasticity diagnostics — the user-written `bpagan` and `whitetst` — are
reproduced through `estat_hettest(…, iid = true)` and `estat_imtest`
respectively, which implement the same `n·R²` LM statistics.
